title: Pneumonia
category: Communicable Disease / Respiratory Infection / Child Health

content.overview:
Pneumonia is an infection that inflames the air sacs (alveoli) in one or both lungs, which can fill with fluid or pus, causing cough, fever, and difficulty breathing. It can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi. Pneumonia is the single largest infectious cause of death in children worldwide, though it affects people of all ages, with the very young, elderly, and immunocompromised at highest risk. It is both preventable (through vaccination and good nutrition) and treatable (with appropriate antibiotics for bacterial pneumonia).

- Pneumonia accounts for 14% of all deaths of children under 5 worldwide, killing over 700,000 children annually.
- In India, pneumonia is one of the leading causes of under-5 mortality.
- Pneumonia can be caused by bacteria (Streptococcus pneumoniae is most common), viruses (RSV, influenza), or fungi.
- Vaccines against Hib, pneumococcus, and measles can prevent pneumonia in children.
- Pneumonia is treatable with antibiotics when bacterial — but access to care and antibiotics remains a challenge in some areas.
- Fast breathing and chest indrawing are the key warning signs of severe pneumonia in children (WHO IMNCI criteria).
- Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months significantly reduces pneumonia risk in infants.
- India's Pneumonia Free India (Social Awareness and Action To Neutralize Pneumonia Successfully — SAANS) campaign targets reducing child pneumonia deaths.

Q: Is pneumonia contagious?
A: The germs that cause pneumonia (bacteria and viruses) can spread through respiratory droplets, but not everyone exposed develops pneumonia — it depends on individual risk factors and immune status.

Q: How can I tell if my child's cold has turned into pneumonia?
A: Watch for fast breathing, chest indrawing (the lower chest pulls in when breathing in), inability to feed, lethargy, or bluish lips. Any of these warrant immediate medical attention — these are the WHO danger signs for severe pneumonia in children.

Q: Do all cases of pneumonia need antibiotics?
A: No. Viral pneumonia does not respond to antibiotics and is managed with supportive care. However, distinguishing bacterial from viral pneumonia often requires clinical judgment by a healthcare provider — never self-medicate with antibiotics.

Q: Can vaccines really prevent pneumonia?
A: Yes. Vaccines against pneumococcus (PCV), Hib, measles, and influenza significantly reduce the risk of pneumonia, especially severe pneumonia in children. These are available free under India's UIP (PCV, Hib, MR).

Q: Why is indoor air pollution linked to pneumonia?
A: Smoke from solid fuel cooking (wood, dung, coal) damages the lining of airways and lungs, making them more susceptible to infection — particularly affecting children and women who spend more time near cooking fires.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
Pneumonia can be life-threatening, especially in young children, the elderly, and people with chronic conditions, but it is preventable and treatable. Recognise the danger signs — fast breathing, chest indrawing, inability to feed, and lethargy in children — and seek immediate medical care. Free vaccines (PCV, Hib, MR) and treatment at government health facilities help prevent and manage pneumonia. Do not delay care if breathing difficulty is present.
types: Bacterial pneumonia: Streptococcus pneumoniae (most common bacterial cause), Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib), Staphylococcus aureus
Viral pneumonia: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) — most common viral cause in young children; influenza viruses; SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19)
Fungal pneumonia: Less common; primarily affects immunocompromised individuals (Pneumocystis jirovecii in HIV/AIDS patients)
Aspiration pneumonia: From inhaling food, liquid, vomit, or saliva into the lungs
Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP): Acquired outside healthcare settings
Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP): Develops during hospital stay (48+ hours after admission)
Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP): Develops in patients on mechanical ventilation
symptoms: Chest pain that worsens with breathing or coughing
Nausea, vomiting, or diarrhoea (more common in children and elderly)
IN CHILDREN — WHO danger signs (severe pneumonia):
Chest indrawing (lower chest wall moves IN when breathing IN — a key danger sign)
IN ELDERLY: May present atypically — confusion, low body temperature instead of fever, general weakness, falls — pneumonia should be considered in any elderly person with sudden decline
warning_signs: Inability to drink/feed, or persistent vomiting
Lethargy, difficult to wake, or unconscious
Severe difficulty breathing or rapid breathing
Confusion or sudden change in mental status
Chest pain that is severe or persistent
Oxygen saturation below 94% (if pulse oximeter available)
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
risk_factors: Age: Children under 5 (especially under 2) and adults over 65
Malnutrition: Significantly increases risk and severity in children
Lack of exclusive breastfeeding in infancy
Incomplete immunisation (Hib, pneumococcal, measles, pertussis vaccines)
Indoor air pollution: Cooking with solid fuels (wood, dung, coal) in poorly ventilated homes — major risk factor in rural India
Chronic diseases: COPD, asthma, diabetes, heart disease, HIV
Weakened immune system (HIV, cancer treatment, steroid use)
Recent viral respiratory infection (flu, COVID-19) — increases susceptibility to bacterial pneumonia
prevention: Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV): Part of India's UIP in most states — prevents pneumococcal pneumonia
Hib vaccine (Pentavalent): Part of UIP — prevents Hib pneumonia and meningitis
Measles vaccine: Measles can lead to severe pneumonia — UIP includes MR/MMR vaccine
Influenza vaccine: Recommended for high-risk groups (elderly, chronic disease patients)
COVID-19 vaccine: Reduces risk of severe COVID pneumonia
Exclusive breastfeeding for first 6 months — provides immune protection
Adequate complementary feeding after 6 months to prevent malnutrition
Use clean cooking fuels (LPG via Ujjwala Yojana) instead of solid fuels
Avoid exposing children to tobacco smoke
Avoid close contact with people who have respiratory infections
environmental_control: Improved kitchen ventilation (chimneys, smokeless chulhas where LPG unavailable)
PMUY (Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana): Provides LPG connections to reduce indoor air pollution exposure
Reducing overcrowding in living spaces where feasible
Avoiding smoking indoors, especially around children and elderly
vaccination: PCV (Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine): 6, 10, 14 weeks (UIP, phased rollout across states)
Influenza: Recommended annually for high-risk groups (not part of UIP)
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
diagnosis: Clinical assessment: Respiratory rate, chest examination (listening for crackles/abnormal sounds), oxygen saturation
Chest X-ray: Confirms presence and extent of lung infection
Blood tests: White blood cell count, blood cultures (to identify bacteria in severe cases)
Sputum culture: To identify causative organism (when available)
Pulse oximetry: Quick, non-invasive measure of oxygen levels — important for severity assessment
treatment_awareness_only: Bacterial pneumonia: Antibiotics — first-line oral antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin for children) for non-severe cases; injectable antibiotics for severe cases requiring hospitalisation
Viral pneumonia: Mainly supportive care (rest, fluids, fever management); antivirals for specific viruses (e.g., oseltamivir for influenza) if appropriate
Oxygen therapy: For low oxygen levels — available at government health facilities, including PHCs equipped under SAANS programme
Severe cases: Hospitalisation, IV antibiotics, oxygen, and close monitoring
Complete the full course of prescribed antibiotics even if symptoms improve
when_to_see_a_doctor: Any child with fast breathing or chest indrawing — seek care immediately (do not wait)
Fever with cough lasting more than 2-3 days, especially with breathing difficulty
Any of the emergency warning signs listed above
Elderly person with sudden confusion, weakness, or decline, even without typical fever/cough
Anyone with chronic disease (COPD, diabetes, heart disease) developing respiratory symptoms
india_specific_context: SAANS (Social Awareness and Action to Neutralize Pneumonia Successfully): National campaign launched by MoHFW to reduce child pneumonia deaths through early identification, referral, and treatment at health facilities
PCV introduction: Phased rollout across Indian states under UIP, prioritising high-burden states
Indoor air pollution from solid fuel use is a major contributor to pneumonia in rural India — PMUY (Ujjwala Yojana) addresses this by promoting LPG use
ASHA workers trained to recognise danger signs (fast breathing, chest indrawing) and refer children promptly
Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY covers hospitalisation for severe pneumonia at empanelled hospitals
Oxygen availability: Strengthened at PHCs and CHCs following COVID-19 pandemic, improving pneumonia care access in rural areas
